title: Caret stability for tokenized contenteditable inputs: preserve selection and reduce jumpiness
preview:
Problem: our tokenized multi-value input (used for recipients, tags, and rule-expressions) relies on contenteditable wrappers for rich behavior. On a set of platforms and edge cases (mobile IME, long paste, browser autofill, and when tokens are recycled during reflow) the caret jumps to the end or selection collapses unexpectedly. This causes frequent user frustration and produces lost input when users are typing quickly or accepting suggested completions.

Goal: define a stable, cross-platform caret-preservation pattern and interaction heuristics for our contenteditable-backed token inputs. Produce design guidance, a small interaction spec for engineering, and an acceptance checklist that prevents regressions. Include fallbacks for browsers where precise caret restores are unreliable and ensure ARIA behavior for screen readers remains correct.

title: Python SDK: event-bridge streaming chat completion ergonomics and retry contract
preview:
Goal:
Improve the Python SDK developer ergonomics for streaming chat completions by introducing an 'event-bridge' abstraction that cleanly surfaces both event-mode and incremental token streams, plus a clearly specified retry/resume contract for transient network and server errors.

Motivation:
Current feedback shows users repeatedly implementing ad-hoc adapters to convert event payloads into higher-level chat messages (for UI) or to reconcile partial tokens after retries. We want a single, well-documented primitive that: 1) provides an async iterator over high-level 'StreamEvent' objects, 2) exposes a low-level raw event stream when needed, 3) carries resume/checkpoint tokens for safe retries, and 4) integrates backpressure semantics compatible with asyncio applications.

Success criteria / Acceptance:
- New API: SDK exposes Chat.stream_events(...) that yields StreamEvent objects with event_type, delta_text, role, token_index, and resume_token fields.
- Clear retry semantics: developers can call .resume_from(token) and the SDK will transparently re-request omitted tokens without duplicating confirmed content (idempotency guarantees documented).
- Backpressure: example patterns use asyncio.Queue with bounded size and demonstrate predictable memory behavior under bursty input.
- Migration: existing .stream_text() remains supported; documentation shows adapter example to switch to StreamEvent for richer UX.
- Tests: integration tests exercising reconnect + resume token path, and unit tests for adapter helpers.

Design notes / proposed API:
- Primary primitive: async for event in client.chat.stream_events(conversation_id=..., model=..., max_queue=64):
    - event is a flat dataclass-like object: {event_type: 'token'|'meta'|'done'|'error', role: 'assistant'|'user'|None, delta: str, token_index: int, resume_token: str}
    - stream_events accepts parameters: timeout, max_queue, on_event middleware hook, and retry_policy name.
- Resume contract: server returns a short opaque resume_token on each committed checkpoint (every N tokens or on sentence boundary). If connection drops, client may call stream_events(resume_from=resume_token). Server must guarantee that resumed stream starts at the first token after the checkpoint (no missing tokens) and that tokens already observed won't be re-emitted unless the model output changed (regression) — SDK will provide a de-duplication helper by token_index.
- Retry behavior: transient errors (5xx, network resets) trigger exponential backoff per retry_policy (default 3 attempts). For non-idempotent actions (function calls, tool invocation) we surface the error and do not auto-retry unless developer opts-in.
- Backpressure/memory: stream_events spawns a single reader task that pushes StreamEvent into an asyncio.Queue bounded by max_queue. Consumers should await queue.get(); if queue is full the reader will apply a configurable policy: drop_oldest | backpressure_block | drop_newest. Default: backpressure_block to avoid silent data loss.

Examples (to include in docs):
- UI-friendly incremental render (async iterator):
    async for ev in client.chat.stream_events(...):
        if ev.event_type == 'token':
            render_token(ev.delta, role=ev.role)
        elif ev.event_type == 'meta':
            update_latency_meter(ev.delta)

- Resume after transient failure:
    last_token = None
    async for ev in client.chat.stream_events(...):
        process(ev)
        last_token = ev.resume_token
    # on connection error, call again with resume_from=last_token

- Adapter to legacy generator API:
    async def stream_text_adapter(*args, **kwargs):
        async for ev in client.chat.stream_events(*args, **kwargs):
            if ev.event_type == 'token':
                yield ev.delta

Trade-offs / open questions:
- How often to emit resume tokens (every token increases overhead, every sentence may delay recovery granularity). Proposal: emit resume token at sentence boundaries and every 64 tokens.
- Should SDK auto-retry beyond 1 request? We default to 3 retries for network errors and leave tool-calls non-retryable. Surface configuration via RetryPolicy.
- Backpressure default: block (simpler) vs drop_oldest (more predictable UI). Chosen default block, but add easy policy switch.

Compatibility:
- Preserve existing streaming text helpers (stream_text) and provide migration guide.
- New StreamEvent object will be a lightweight namedtuple-like dict for backwards compatibility with type checks.

Linking artifacts:
- Design doc: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/SDK/pages/12345/py-event-bridge-design
- Draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-python/pull/482
- Related Linear tasks: ENG-19876 (streaming consumer patterns), ENG-31247 (stream resume/splitter)

Implementation plan:
1) Prototype event-bridge in feature branch and add adapter layer. (owner: Ethan)
2) Add integration test harness that simulates partial token emission and network dropouts. (owner: QA)
3) Docs and migration guide with two concrete examples (UI and server-side consumer). (owner: DevRel)
4) Feature flag rollout and beta release, solicit feedback from 10 early customers.

Observability / Telemetry:
- Emit SDK-level telemetry events when: resume attempted, resume succeeded, retry exhausted, queue full/backpressure applied. These should be opt-in via sdk telemetry config.

Acceptance test checklist:
- [ ] stream_events yields tokens in-order across a simulated disconnect+resume
- [ ] resume_token semantics validated (no duplicates beyond de-dup window)
- [ ] backpressure behavior reproducible with max_queue=2 in test harness
- [ ] docs and migration examples published
- [ ] integration test added to CI

title: Stream pipeline: context propagation and early termination ergonomics for Go SDK
preview:
Problem statement: The current Go SDK stream surface does not make it easy for callers to reliably abort in-flight streams, propagate deadlines to internal goroutines, or observe partial results when an operation is terminated early. This has resulted in customer reports of leaked goroutines during canceled requests, unclear semantics when context deadlines elapse during streaming, and poor ergonomics for common patterns (timeout per-chunk vs overall deadline).

Goal: Rework the streaming implementation and public ergonomics to provide a predictable pipeline model that: 1) reliably propagates cancellation and deadlines to all internal workers; 2) supports early termination with deterministic cleanup; 3) exposes a small set of helpers/examples for common timeouts (per-chunk, overall, and graceful drain); and 4) preserves/respects retry semantics for transient connection errors without violating user deadlines.

Scope: This ticket covers the Go SDK runtime-side changes and API ergonomics (no server changes). Work includes internal pipeline refactor, new helper utilities, unit/integration tests, examples, and docs. It intentionally does not implement resumable checkpointing for disconnected clients — that will be a follow-up.

Non-goals: Changing server-side streaming protocol, adding new top-level API flavors (we keep Recv/RecvChunk style), or introducing a different concurrency model (still goroutine + channels).

Key user scenarios we must be correct for: 1) Caller uses context.WithTimeout for overall request; stream must stop and return ctx.Err() quickly and not leak goroutines. 2) Caller wants to perform short per-chunk timeouts (e.g., wait 200ms for next chunk, otherwise abort) and still observe already-received chunks. 3) Network flaps cause a transient transport error: client should attempt limited reconnect/retry but not extend user deadline. 4) Caller cancels due to UI action: SDK should best-effort flush buffered chunks and close underlying connection gracefully.

title: Raftless throttler drain + sticky fairness protocol for limiter outage recovery
preview: Implement a lightweight, non-consensus (raftless) drain and recovery mode for token/lease-based throttlers to maintain sticky fairness during limiter outages and cross-region failovers. Goal: ensure bounded customer impact during limiter restarts, network partitions, and regional circuit-breaker activations while keeping 99th percentile latency and error SLOs intact for premium tenants.

title: Go SDK model-mapper, validators and streaming facade
preview: Goal: Add a minimal, ergonomic layer to the Go SDK that 1) maps raw generation envelopes into strongly-typed model structs using a small set of generics helpers, 2) validates structured output contracts at decode time with pluggable validation rules, and 3) provides a streaming facade (async iterator-like) that exposes decoded items and surface-level backpressure controls for callers. This ticket focuses on API shape, reference implementation, and examples (not final perf tuning).

title: Tenant-aware observability pipeline: deterministic pseudonymization, retention enforcer, and access audit
preview: Build a tenant-aware observability ingestion and downstream pipeline that removes or pseudonymizes tenant-sensitive fields deterministically, enforces retention at the dataset/tenant level, and produces cryptographic access audit trails. This ticket scopes the core pipeline and the integration points needed for tracing hooks, metric/log separation, and the dashboard-level access audit views. The goal is to allow product teams to continue troubleshooting with meaningful telemetry while ensuring PII is protected and access to raw identifiers is auditable and controllable.

title: Trace Ingest Translator: infer-schema and enrich spans for mixed OTLP/Zipkin clients
preview:
Background:
We ingest spans from a wide variety of clients: OTLP native SDKs, legacy Zipkin/Jaeger shims, and several internal SDK versions that emit nonstandard attributes. Downstream backends (Tempo, legacy Jaeger sinks) expect a relatively consistent schema for trace_id/span_id/timestamps and common attributes (service.name, http.method, db.statement, tenant).

Goal:
Build a lightweight Trace Ingest Translator component in the ingest path that: 1) infers and normalizes span schema from incoming envelopes, 2) applies deterministic attribute mapping and enrichment rules, 3) preserves tenant and sampling metadata, and 4) outputs a canonical OTLP-like envelope to Tempo and the Jaeger compatibility writer. The translator must be low-latency (target median added latency < 6ms per-span at p95 < 12ms under typical load) and horizontally sharding-friendly.

Scope / Non-goals:
- Include only ingest-time normalization and enrichment (no heavy enrichment calls to external services).
- Not responsible for long-term archival or reprocessing; raw envelopes will still be persisted to S3 for replays.
- Does not replace downstream schema migration work; it's an interoperability shim.

Design decisions made so far:
- Inference heuristic: prefer OTLP field names when present; fallback rules map common Zipkin tags ("http.method", "http.path", "serviceName") into canonical keys.
- Timestamp correction: if span.start is zero or < 2000-01-01, attempt to reconstruct from arrival_time - duration; otherwise mark span with "redwood.ingest.timestamp_uncertain" attribute.
- Attribute normalization: lowercase keys, strip vendor prefixes (e.g., "com.vendor.foo" => "foo"), and collapse equivalent tags via a mapping table stored in a config map (editable via Console).
- High-cardinality keys (user_id, session_id) are flagged and not forwarded to some downstream aggregators; they are preserved in raw archives and optionally forwarded to Tempo behind a feature flag.
- Tenant mapping: accept multiple tenant hints (auth header, envelope.tenant, host header) in priority order; if none available, tag as "tenant:unknown" and route to a quarantine lane for manual review.

Acceptance criteria / success metrics:
- All test vectors (100+ variations of OTLP/Zipkin/Jaeger envelopes) normalize to expected canonical output with an automated golden-file test.
- End-to-end p50 added latency by translator < 6ms at steady-state 25k spans/s; p95 < 12ms for a 3-node translator cluster.
- No more than 0.05% of spans placed in quarantine for schema errors after first week of canary.
- Metrics emitted: translator.normalized_spans.count, translator.dropped_spans.count, translator.latency_ms.p50/p95, translator.remap_conflicts.count.

Implementation plan:
1) Prototype a single-process translator service (Go) using current otel proto structs for parsing and a small rule engine for mapping. (ENG-345678-1)
2) Build golden test harness and CI job with corpus of historical envelopes collected from staging and customer replay. (ENG-345678-2)
3) Implement sharding key: tenant_id if present else hash(service.name|host). Support consistent hashing to allow sticky caches for KV lookups/enrichments. (ENG-345678-3)
4) Add Console config for attribute mapping table and a small UI to preview transformations for a sample envelope. (ENG-345678-4)
5) Canary in us-west-2 ingest path behind a rollout flag; mirror traffic to existing path for validation. Monitor metrics and error traces for 72h before progressive rollout. (ENG-345678-5)

Rollout & fallback:
- Stage 0: shadow mode only (translator runs but outputs are ignored by the live path). Compare translator output to current path and flag diffs.
- Stage 1: Canary traffic (5%): translator output used for a subset of tenants heavy traffic; fallback to legacy pipeline on mismatch.
- Stage 2: Gradual ramp to 100% with automated rollback if remap_conflicts.rate > 0.1% over 30m window.

Tests and validation:
- Unit tests for mapping engine, timestamp heuristics, and tenant extraction.
- Golden-file integration tests for 120 envelope variants.
- Load test harness at 100k spans/s to determine memory/CPU profiles for autoscaling policies (target 25k spans/s per instance).

Observability / Dashboards:
- New dashboard: "Translator — Health & Integrity" (panels: normalized vs raw diffs, latency histogram, quarantine count, remap_conflicts trend).
- Probe: add an internal tracer span for each batch through translator to trace sampling and latency breakdown.

Docs & PRs:
- Confluence spec: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/OBS/pages/Trace+Ingest+Translator+Spec
- Prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/ingest/pull/1442
- Golden tests: https://github.com/redwood-inference/ingest-tests/compare/translator-golden

Open questions / trade-offs:
- How aggressive should we be collapsing numeric ID keys? (risk: breaking user-level debugging but reduces cardinality). Proposal: configurable per-tenant policy with safe defaults that preserve IDs for VIP tenants.
- Should we add enrichment lookups (e.g., resolve service.display_name) via a cached service registry? We plan to keep enrichment local-cache only and add a background refresher to avoid blocking the critical path.

Next steps (short-term):
- Finish prototype and golden test harness (Owners: @aisha, @ethan) — ETA 2026-03-20.
- Run initial canary in staging and collect diffs for product/SE review.
- Open PR for Console mapping UI prototype (small read-only preview) for PM/design feedback.

- Inference heuristic: prefer OTLP field names when present; fallback rules map common Zipkin tags ("http.method", "http.path", "serviceName") into canonical keys.
- Timestamp correction: if span.start is zero or < 2000-01-01, attempt to reconstruct from arrival_time - duration; otherwise mark span with "redwood.ingest.timestamp_uncertain" attribute.
- Attribute normalization: lowercase keys, strip vendor prefixes (e.g., "com.vendor.foo" => "foo"), and collapse equivalent tags via a mapping table stored in a config map (editable via Console).
- High-cardinality keys (user_id, session_id) are flagged and not forwarded to some downstream aggregators; they are preserved in raw archives and optionally forwarded to Tempo behind a feature flag.
- Tenant mapping: accept multiple tenant hints (auth header, envelope.tenant, host header) in priority order; if none available, tag as "tenant:unknown" and route to a quarantine lane for manual review.

Acceptance criteria / success metrics:
- All test vectors (100+ variations of OTLP/Zipkin/Jaeger envelopes) normalize to expected canonical output with an automated golden-file test.
- End-to-end p50 added latency by translator < 6ms at steady-state 25k spans/s; p95 < 12ms for a 3-node translator cluster.
- No more than 0.05% of spans placed in quarantine for schema errors after first week of canary.
- Metrics emitted: translator.normalized_spans.count, translator.dropped_spans.count, translator.latency_ms.p50/p95, translator.remap_conflicts.count.

Implementation plan:
1) Prototype a single-process translator service (Go) using current otel proto structs for parsing and a small rule engine for mapping. (ENG-345678-1)
2) Build golden test harness and CI job with corpus of historical envelopes collected from staging and customer replay. (ENG-345678-2)
3) Implement sharding key: tenant_id if present else hash(service.name|host). Support consistent hashing to allow sticky caches for KV lookups/enrichments. (ENG-345678-3)
4) Add Console config for attribute mapping table and a small UI to preview transformations for a sample envelope. (ENG-345678-4)
5) Canary in us-west-2 ingest path behind a rollout flag; mirror traffic to existing path for validation. Monitor metrics and error traces for 72h before progressive rollout. (ENG-345678-5)

Rollout & fallback:
- Stage 0: shadow mode only (translator runs but outputs are ignored by the live path). Compare translator output to current path and flag diffs.
- Stage 1: Canary traffic (5%): translator output used for a subset of tenants heavy traffic; fallback to legacy pipeline on mismatch.
- Stage 2: Gradual ramp to 100% with automated rollback if remap_conflicts.rate > 0.1% over 30m window.

Tests and validation:
- Unit tests for mapping engine, timestamp heuristics, and tenant extraction.
- Golden-file integration tests for 120 envelope variants.
- Load test harness at 100k spans/s to determine memory/CPU profiles for autoscaling policies (target 25k spans/s per instance).

Observability / Dashboards:
- New dashboard: "Translator — Health & Integrity" (panels: normalized vs raw diffs, latency histogram, quarantine count, remap_conflicts trend).
- Probe: add an internal tracer span for each batch through translator to trace sampling and latency breakdown.

Docs & PRs:
- Confluence spec: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/OBS/pages/Trace+Ingest+Translator+Spec
- Prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/ingest/pull/1442
- Golden tests: https://github.com/redwood-inference/ingest-tests/compare/translator-golden

Open questions / trade-offs:
- How aggressive should we be collapsing numeric ID keys? (risk: breaking user-level debugging but reduces cardinality). Proposal: configurable per-tenant policy with safe defaults that preserve IDs for VIP tenants.
- Should we add enrichment lookups (e.g., resolve service.display_name) via a cached service registry? We plan to keep enrichment local-cache only and add a background refresher to avoid blocking the critical path.

Next steps (short-term):
- Finish prototype and golden test harness (Owners: @aisha, @ethan) — ETA 2026-03-20.
- Run initial canary in staging and collect diffs for product/SE review.
- Open PR for Console mapping UI prototype (small read-only preview) for PM/design feedback.

title: Auth Observability After‑Action: RBACv2, SSO & Audit Pipeline SLOs/Alerts Remediation
preview:
Summary: This ticket consolidates findings from the March 2026 auth/audit availability incident and defines a prioritized remediation plan across three areas: RBACv2 policy evaluation latency, SSO/SAML session handling under spike load, and the audit-event delivery pipeline. The goal is to make the auth surface observable and resilient with concrete SLOs and tuned alerting so regressions are caught earlier and with actionable signals.

What triggered this: A region-level capacity spike caused transient delays in token validation and SAML assertions leading to failed sign-ins and delayed audit-event ingestion. Multiple signals were noisy (high auth latency, partial 502s from the auth proxy, and audit sink lag), which slowed incident diagnosis.

High-level proposed outcomes: 1) Define and publish auth SLOs and error budgets; 2) Reduce blind spots by adding end-to-end observability for RBAC eval timing and SAML hops; 3) Rationalize and tune alerts to reduce paging for known low‑impact noise; 4) Implement prioritized fixes for audit pipeline delivery reliability.

SLO examples to adopt (targets are initial proposals to be iterated):
- Auth API latency: 99th percentile <= 200ms (token validation + RBAC eval)
- Auth API availability: 99.95% (errors resulting in user-facing failures)
- Audit event delivery: 99.5% delivered to primary sink within 30s
- SSO login end-to-end success rate: 99.9% over 24h window

Rationale: These targets align with platform SLAs and aim to prioritize user-visible failures first. Audit delivery SLO is slightly lower given queueing characteristics but must be observable so integrity concerns are caught quickly.

title: Reusable spark-band component with ARIA summary and lazy aggregation
preview:
Problem: Several dashboards rely on bespoke sparkline and band components implemented inconsistently across products. This leads to duplicated logic, accessibility variance (different aria summaries), and performance regressions when many small charts render on the same page.

Goal: Design a single, reusable 'spark-band' primitive optimized for dense time-series displays. It should: (1) provide a concise ARIA summary for screen readers, (2) expose a small API for visual and interaction variants (line, area, band, micro-bar), (3) support lazy aggregation/decimation for high-point series, (4) adopt tokens for color/spacing, and (5) include a Canvas-first implementation with an accessible DOM fallback for legacy browsers/screen readers.

title: Consolidate surface tokens and compact variants for dense dataviz kit
preview: Problem: The DataViz surface kit (buttons, table rows, chart legends, small density cards) has grown a high-entropy set of visual-only variants and duplicated surface tokens leading to publish failures, inconsistent runtime spacing, and bloated instance counts. Goal: Reduce variant entropy by collapsing visually redundant variants into token-driven states, normalize token names to the code spec, and add a non-destructive publish pipeline with preflight checks and a targeted instance migration plan. This work should enable predictable library publishes, make auto-layout constraints resilient for responsive breakpoints, and reduce downstream engineering churn when mapping Figma tokens to CSS/Design tokens. Deliverables: 1) Audit spreadsheet of all surface tokens and variants across DataViz libraries. 2) Variant compression plan that documents which visual differences become tokens vs which remain variants. 3) Figma library changes (renamed tokens, archived variants, new alias tokens) and a migration script/recipe for instances. 4) Publish preflight checklist and CI gate to prevent regressions. 5) Rollout plan with canary teams and opt-in migration window.

title: Tiered fidelity, spoken briefs, and event coalescing for timeline/series charts
preview:
Goal: Define a design spec and interaction pattern for time-series timeline charts that reduces CPU/GPU work under constrained conditions while preserving a useful, accessible summary for keyboard and screen-reader users. This ticket covers three coordinated pieces: 1) visual token migration to support fidelity profiles, 2) a spoken-briefs accessibility mode (concise TTS summaries on focus/hovers), and 3) event coalescing rules to limit frequent re-rendering during high-frequency input (scrubbing/rapid live updates).

Why now: Observability dashboards and mobile customers report spikes in rendering cost and janky hovers when streams update at >10Hz or users scrub quickly. We need a predictable degradation strategy that product teams can opt into and designers and devs can implement consistently across chart components.

Scope: Design system tokens and component mixins for three fidelity tiers (high, balanced, battery-saver), UX copy for spoken briefs and sr-only summaries, hit-testing/interaction layering spec (separate lightweight hit layer), and rules for coalescing inbound events (time-window and magnitude thresholds). Prototype interactions in Figma and provide acceptance criteria and a dev checklist for ENG.

Acceptance criteria: 
- Visual token set defined and mapped to fidelity tiers (color, stroke width, annotation opacity, sampling thresholds).
- Figma prototype demonstrates transitions between tiers at 60s and 2s simulated load windows.
- Spoken-briefs copy and SR markup documented: keyboard focus and pivot announcements for aggregates (min/max/median) with explicit examples for 5, 30, and 120-second windows.
- Hit-testing spec with a lightweight pointer layer described; examples for hover vs click semantics and failover to keyboard probe.
- Event-coalescing algorithm specified: default coalescing window = 150ms with magnitude threshold ±0.5% or configurable per-chart; examples of synthetic input streams and resulting re-render rate.
- Implementation checklist for ENG including token migration guide, required props/API hooks, and telemetry signals to emit (render-time, dropped-events, spoken-brief-trigger).

Out of scope: Backend sampling algorithms, low-level kernel scheduling in runtime, and changes to existing chart data-models beyond adding config flags and telemetry hooks.

title: Adaptive surface layer and shadow geometry update
preview:
Problem: Our current token set treats elevation as a single numeric scale with static shadows and fixed surface colors. This leads to inconsistent perceived depth across components (tables, dialogs, cards) and creates friction when implementing dense vs. spacious product variants.

Goal: Produce an updated tokens spec that introduces: (1) adaptive surface layers that shift tone based on container context and elevation class; (2) shadow geometry tokens that describe directional light, umbra/penumbra scaling and micro-elevation offsets; (3) responsive radius presets tied to density breakpoints; (4) an aligned typography mapping for headline/body scales that better matches cap-height across sizes. The result should reduce per-component overrides, improve contrast in both light/dark modes, and make elevation feel consistent under variable densities.

Scope: tokens.json changes, component token mapping (buttons, tables, charts, dialogs, popovers), updated Sketch/Figma styles, design docs and code examples for frontend engineers, and QA checklist for accessibility contrast and motion.

Non-goals: This ticket does not include runtime batching changes, nor does it attempt to change color semantics for brand accents beyond token variables.

Success metrics: fewer component-specific elevation overrides in PRs, automated visual diff pass rates improve by 40% on affected screens, accessibility contrast >= WCAG AA for surfaces and text at defined sizes.

title: Dynamic slew-rate limiter with progressive KV compaction to mitigate queue pileup at sustained high QPS
preview:
Background: During recent weekend load tests and two customer incidents we observed sustained high QPS (50k+ req/s across the cluster) producing prolonged microbatch queue growth, increased tail latency, and KV cache pressure that eventually triggers out-of-memory (OOM) evictions and degraded throughput. Existing admission heuristics (static rate caps + simple headroom checks) react too late or oscillate under nonstationary traffic patterns.

Goal: Implement a coordinated admission and memory-reclamation strategy that prevents queue pileups before OOMs occur while preserving throughput and KV hit rate. Specifically: introduce a 'slew-rate limiter' to throttle the entrance of new microbatches smoothly and a progressive KV compaction policy to reclaim low-value cache entries when memory headroom falls below safe thresholds. The two mechanisms should be tenant-aware and integrate with existing autoscaling and fallback routing policies.

title: Output contract monitoring and rollout safety for shadow & canary traffic
preview:
Problem:
Teams running model rollouts need deterministic, low-friction checks to ensure new model variants do not break structured-output contracts or tool-calling behavior when traffic is gradually shifted (shadow, canary, A/B). Today we detect regressions late via downstream errors and manual rollbacks.

Goal:
- Provide a runtime-compatible set of validators and diffing tools that run on shadowed and canary traffic to detect structured-output incompatibilities (broken schemas, missing required fields, unexpected types) and tool-call signature regressions (changed function names, param lists, arg types).
- Surface regression metrics in the Console and trigger automated rollback when contract-violation thresholds are exceeded, while minimizing false positives.

Proposed approach:
1) Contract Registry: keep a versioned, lightweight schema/contract for each route/structured-output and for each registered tool/function signature. Contracts are simple JSON Schema-ish declarations (type, required, enum, shape hints) stored in the control plane.
2) Deterministic Validators: runtime validation service that takes model output (and tool-call metadata) and classifies as OK / Warning / Violation. Implement fast checks in the request path for shadowed traffic (async) and in the canary path with sampling.
3) Delta Diffs: compute a compact diff between baseline (pinned stable) model output and candidate output for the same prompts. Diff categories: structural (missing/extra fields), type drift, enum drift, and semantic collisions (via light heuristics). Persist aggregated diffs per rollout window.
4) Metricization & Thresholds: expose violation rate, severity-weighted score, and `breaking-change` boolean derived from contract violations + downstream error rate. Allow release-level thresholds (e.g., auto-rollback if breaking-change rate > 1% over 5m AND error-rate increase > 0.5%).
5) Auto-Rollback: integrate with rollout controller to automatically stop or roll back a canary when thresholds breach. Provide an opt-in per-customer or per-route guard.
6) Noise reduction & exceptions: allow route-level exceptions (non-fatal fields), fuzzy type coercions, and a sample-based golden-set of prompts to reduce flakiness.

Acceptance criteria:
- Validator runs in shadow mode for all canary rollouts with <10ms median overhead to main path (async allowed).
- Console shows per-rollout contract-violation rate and diff breakdown within 60s of aggregate window end.
- Auto-rollback triggers correctly in simulated tests (see PRD test harness) and respects opt-out flags.
- Documented developer workflow for adding/updating contracts, and an SDK helper to create function signature manifests.

Trade-offs considered:
- Strict schema enforcement causes false positives for models that intentionally change shape (we mitigate with a golden-set and human review flows).
- Storing large example outputs for diffs is costly; we aggregate diffs and keep only sampled raw traces.
- Runtime validation complexity vs speed: prioritize async validation for high-volume routes and sample-based sync checks for canaries where latency SLOs matter.

title: Guided POP routing with quantile-differential and link-safety score
preview:
Problem statement: We need a region/POP (Point-of-Presence) assignment policy that is resilient to transient spikes that inflate p95 while preserving tail SLOs, and that can also rapidly avoid links showing degraded packet-loss or high retransmit rates. Existing approaches overreact to short-lived p95 excursions or ignore cross-metric correlation.

Proposal summary: Implement a 'quantile-differential with link-safety' guider that computes a composite preference score per POP combining: (1) smoothed p50 and p95 (EWMA windowed), (2) the p95-p50 delta (as a sensitivity factor), and (3) a network link-safety penalty derived from loss/rtx/icmp-failure telemetry. The guider will provide ranked POP choices and a binary failover trigger when safety thresholds breach. It prioritizes stable low-latency POPs but gracefully shifts when tail-risk or network degradation is sustained.

Algorithm (high level):
- Maintain two EWMAs per POP: EWMA50 (p50) and EWMA95 (p95) with different alphas (alpha50 >> alpha95).
- Compute quantile_delta = max(0, EWMA95 - EWMA50).
- Normalize latency_score = sigmoid((target_p50 - EWMA50) / sigma) to map lower-latency to higher score.
- Compute stability_penalty = clamp(quantile_delta / q_threshold, 0, 1).
- Compute link_safety_penalty = clamp( loss_rate*Lw + retransmit_rate*Rw + icmp_error*Iw, 0, 1 ). Weights (Lw,Rw,Iw) configured per-region.
- Composite_score = latency_score * (1 - stability_penalty) * (1 - link_safety_penalty).
- If link_safety_penalty > failover_cutoff OR EWMA95 > hard_latency_cutoff for duration T_fail, mark POP as degraded and exclude for N_cooldown seconds.

Why this is different: Instead of raw percentiles or single-threshold circuit breakers, this approach uses the quantile gap as an explicit signal of tail instability and blends it with independent network health metrics to avoid routing towards POPs with packet-level problems. It also provides tunable sensitivity so SREs can choose how aggressive failovers are for different customer tiers.

title: Centralize GPU launch templates and autoscaler integration for Private VPC
preview:
Background: Private deployments require a repeatable, auditable Terraform module that produces GPU-capable node groups for both ASG-based autoscaling and Karpenter-driven provisioning. Current infra scripts are duplicated across repos causing drift in launch template settings (IMDS, ENA drivers, GPU kernel modules, kubelet flags) and discrepant health-check behaviors between ASG and Karpenter nodes.

Goal: Create a single Terraform module (private_gpu_node_group) that centralizes launch template generation, user-data bootstrapping, AMI lookup, and autoscaler-compatible tags/labels so we can:
- Provision ASG-backed GPU node groups in VPC-private clusters with consistent health checks and lifecycle hooks.
- Provision Karpenter-compatible instance profiles and launch configuration artifacts so Karpenter can provision GPU nodes with identical bootstrapping.
- Enable seamless in-place upgrades of launch templates and node pools with minimal downtime.
- Expose configurable knobs for instance types, gpu_driver_version, kernel_module_install, kubelet_opts, taints/labels, and node-level monitoring.

Scope: Implement Terraform module with:
- Centralized launch_template resource and userdata templating using templatefile + S3-baked assets.
- Support for both ASG (aws_autoscaling_group + lifecycle hooks) and Karpenter (aws_launch_template + instance profile + capacity_type tags).
- Health checks that integrate with cluster-autoscaler/Karpenter expectations: node readiness probes, kubelet systemd restarts, and EC2 status checks.
- Integration tests (terraform plan/apply) in sandbox account and smoke tests in a private cluster.

Non-goals: Rewriting node image build pipelines (we'll consume existing AMI pipeline), replacing Karpenter upstream controller behavior, or changing customer-facing API surface for Private deployments.

Design notes / trade-offs:
- Centralized launch template simplifies auditability but requires a careful upgrade path: we will implement versioned launch templates and automated rolling replacement using kube-drain + ASG instance refresh or Karpenter node termination hooks.
- For ASG, lifecycle hooks will run a pre-terminate script to drain pods and push node metrics. For Karpenter, we'll rely on annotated termination handlers. This duplicates some logic but keeps each autoscaler’s semantics intact.
- To avoid IAM bloat, instance profiles will be templated and optionally reused across node groups.
- User-data will prefer small, idempotent bootstrap scripts that fetch the latest GPU driver artifacts from a signed S3 bucket (locked by KMS).

Acceptance criteria:
- A Terraform module under modules/private_gpu_node_group that can be consumed by Private deployment stacks and supports a boolean flag to choose ASG vs Karpenter semantics.
- Module outputs: launch_template_id, instance_profile_arn, asg_name (if ASG), karpenter_provisioner_manifest_snippet (if Karpenter).
- Automated infra tests: terraform plan/apply in sandbox and a CI job that deploys a small cluster, schedules GPU workload, and verifies node readiness + GPU device visibility.
- Upgrade path documented: step-by-step runbook for rolling update using node replacements with example commands and expected downtime metrics.
- Health checks: nodes with kubelet not ready for >5m trigger alert; EC2 status failing triggers ASG replacement and Karpenter reprovisioning.

Rollout plan:
1) Merge module into infra repo behind feature flag.
2) Smoke deploy to staging Private cluster (non-customer).
3) Enable in a single low-traffic customer VPC with monitoring and rollback playbook.
4) Gradual ramp across Private customers.

Testing/Validation:
- Unit: terraform validate + fmt checks.
- Integration: sandbox apply that creates ASG and Karpenter provisioner variants, runs a GPU tensor workload, and verifies metrics and healthcheck behavior.
- Load: simulate scale-up events to validate Karpenter provisioning latency and ASG warm pool behavior.

Docs / artifacts required:
- Confluence: PRD and runbook for upgrades (link in attachments).
- GH: module repo + example stacks (PR numbers in comments).
- Dashboard: add dashboards for node-level GPU errors and ASG instance refresh durations.

Open questions:
- Should we bake GPU drivers into AMIs or continue runtime install in user-data? (trade-off: faster boot vs AMI management complexity)
- Aligning lifecycle/termination semantics between ASG and Karpenter: acceptable to keep separate implementations?

Success metrics:
- Eliminate >90% of launch-template drift across Private repos.
- Reduce node replacement-induced customer errors during upgrades by 50% in the first two months after rollout.

title: Python SDK: unified streaming sink + resume hooks for chat completions
preview:
Problem summary: Users building streaming chat integrations with the Python SDK are facing three recurring pain points: 1) scattered callback shapes for incremental tokens vs structured events, 2) lack of a small, composable contract for checkpoint/resume semantics (needed for flaky networks and long-lived conversations), and 3) unclear retry/idempotency behavior when applying partial outputs to user state.

This ticket proposes a lightweight "unified streaming sink" API and resume hooks that: (a) normalize event shapes (start, delta, tool_call, structured_output, end, error, meta), (b) expose a checkpoint/resume contract with user-provided idempotency keys and explicit boundaries, and (c) specify retry semantics with guidance and helpers for common app patterns (UI streaming, transcript persistence, server-side reruns). The goal is ergonomic defaults plus explicit escape hatches for advanced producers/consumers.

Deliverables include: a documented chain-of-custody for usage events, a retention schedule mapped to legal and commercial requirements, an explicit least-privilege access matrix for services and people, and automated artifacts (signed snapshots, tamper-evident manifests) that can be exported to auditors.

title: Traffic fencing and model admission controller for heterogeneous environments
preview:
Problem: We lack a single, lightweight mechanism to safely admit and fence model traffic across Hosted, Dedicated and Private installs. Existing rollout tooling assumes uniform networking and capacity semantics; Private/VPC and Dedicated clusters impose per-tenant quotas, stricter egress rules, and slower kernel upgrade windows. That mismatch has caused noisy canaries and manual intervention during several recent model promotions.

Goal: Build a model admission controller + traffic fencing primitives that: 1) enforces per-environment constraints (capacity, network policy, KMS availability), 2) performs fast local rejection for incompatible requests, 3) integrates with canary/A-B/shadow flows to prevent noisy experiments from spilling into production, and 4) emits clear signals for automated rollback and human ops. The controller must be lightweight (config-first), pluggable into Redwood routing layer, and backward compatible with existing rollout APIs.

title: Stochastic arg-fuzz eval harness for function selection precision
preview: Goal: Build an automated, stochastic evaluation harness that detects regressions in function/tool selection accuracy and argument/schema validation after model, runtime, or config changes. This harness will exercise the tool-calling surface by generating randomized prompt variants, sampling model responses, performing schema fuzzing on arguments, and measuring selection precision, false positive invocations, and argument conformance. The output should be a reproducible daily job, per-commit checks for PRs touching routing/config, and a dashboard with drift alerts and example failures for triage.

title: Routing decision audit trail and per-hop telemetry enhancements
preview: Scope: Add machine-readable decision logs and sampled distributed traces for the smart routing layer that performs region selection, tier routing, and fallback to model variants. Provide per-hop metrics (latency, queue depth, token count) emitted at each routing hop (edge proxy -> router -> model-host) and correlate them by a trace_id and decision_id. Deliverables: 1) decision_log schema and storage plan (low-cost cold storage + short-term hot index), 2) cross-service trace propagation and instrumentation, 3) per-hop metrics with aggregation keys for route_policy, model_variant, region, tier, and customer_tier, 4) dashboards and alerting for anomalous routing patterns, and 5) sampling & redaction policy to maintain privacy and SLOs. Non-goal: full request payload retention; only metadata and short decision fingerprints retained per policy.

title: Inbound open-model risk bucketing and staged rollout contract
preview: Clarify requirements for how new open (community) model submissions are classified by risk, what metadata gates they must include, and the rollout contract that binds pinning, telemetry gating, and staged percentage exposure. This PRD fragment is intended to: 1) define discrete risk buckets (Low/Moderate/High) with concrete signal thresholds; 2) specify minimum intake metadata required to accept an unpinned variant; 3) prescribe a staged rollout plan with telemetry gates and automatic rollback triggers; and 4) document operator responsibilities and handoffs to SRE/security for final admission. Goal is to give engineering clear pass/fail criteria for the intake pipeline and a deterministic rollout contract for Catalog and Runtime teams.

title: Client onramp prioritization rubric — language coverage, auth patterns & streaming ergonomics
preview:
Problem: We receive a steady stream of customer requests for new language client libraries and auth/streaming ergonomics changes. Without a consistent intake and prioritization rubric we over-index on vocal requests and create engineering churn. This ticket defines a repeatable rubric and intake flow for triaging SDK/client requests with a focus on enterprise needs (auth patterns, token rotation, SSO/OIDC, resumable streaming, backpressure) and language coverage trade-offs.

Goal: Establish a lightweight scoring rubric, an intake form, and a small gating checklist so PM/Eng/Design can rapidly decide: Build native SDK, provide official adapter, or document a community pattern. Deliverables:
- Prioritization rubric (weighted scores and thresholds)
- Intake form template for Solutions/Support/AE to capture signals
- Triage runbook with owners and SLAs (48h acknowledge, 10 business days initial decision)
- Example triage outcomes for 3 representative customer asks (PHP client for streaming inference; Rust production client with mTLS + token rotation; mobile JS with resumable reconnect)

Scope (what this ticket covers):
- Language coverage decision criteria (usage signals, revenue linked customers, engineering cost, runtime compatibility)
- Auth pattern requirements: token refresh flows, client credentials, SSO/OIDC federation, mTLS for private deployments, KMS/HSM considerations for Private customers
- Streaming ergonomics: reconnect/resume semantics, backpressure and chunked responses, metadata propagation, idempotency and delivery guarantees
- Non-goals: Implementation of any single SDK (those will be follow-up tickets if rubric leads to build)

preview: Create an operational playbook and implementation ticket set to choose, validate, and publish per-model quantization presets (FP8/INT8) in the model catalog with automated calibration runs and accuracy validation. The goal is to provide safe, documented defaults that ship with each catalog entry, plus automated guardrails and rollback behavior so customers get predictable quality/cost trade-offs without manual experimentation.

title: Performance Studio: Per-Model and Per-Route Token Flow + Cost Layering Spec
preview: Goal: Define a compact, scannable ‘Performance Studio’ view that surfaces per-model and per-route token flow, latency distribution, throughput, and cost layers in a single composable canvas. Deliverables: 1) chart specs for Token Stream (waterfall + micro-lanes), Token Rate (sparklines + histogram), Latency Distribution (p99/p95/p50 bands + kernel overlay), Throughput heatmap, and Cost Layering (stacked area that maps batching/quantization/cache savings to monetary units); 2) interaction patterns (time windowing, zoom, trace linkouts, model variant toggle); 3) accessibility and performance constraints; 4) annotated mockups and a Figma prototype for designer/PM/eng review. Scope: Per-route drilldown to show the top 5 model variants serving that route, token-level breakdown (prompt vs. generation vs. system tokens), and a cost attribution view that ties token segments to estimated cloud/GPU cost and Redwood unit cost. We will support two roll-up granularities: 1-minute (default) and 15-second (on-demand sampling). Data sources are telemetry pipelines (route metrics, model execution traces, cost oracle). This spec intentionally prioritizes readability for on-call engineers while retaining the depth needed by platform/product owners.

title: Foldable Cost Attribution Tiles — prototype validation, redlines, and stakeholder playback
preview:
Summary: This ticket captures the design review cycle for the new foldable (accordion-style) cost attribution tiles meant for the Console overview and rollout detail pages. The goal is to provide a compact, scannable cost summary that expands to reveal attribution slices, cost drivers, and quick-actions (drilldown, compare, pin).

What we prototyped: A lightweight tile with three microstates — collapsed, summary-expanded (inline list + miniature sparkline), and full-expanded (detailed attribution graph + filter controls). Prototype: Figma link (internal): https://redwood.design/figma/foldable-cost-tiles (private). Playback recording: https://drive.redwood.com/recordings/cost-foldables-2026-02-26.mp4.

Stakeholder feedback (synthesis):
- PM (Aisha Rahman): Loves compact density but wants a clear signal when data is sampled vs. exact. Request: add a badge for "estimated" vs "exact" for reduced-support confusion.
- Engineering (Diego Alvarez): Concern about layout shift and reflow when expanding many tiles in a list. Suggests using transform/opacity-based animation and deferring heavy charts until after interaction.
- SRE/Performance: Prefer progressive reveal for heavy charting (lazy-load on first open). Also flagged potential memory regression for pages with 200+ tiles.
- Design leadership (Lena Ortiz): Request stronger affordance for keyboard users and a motion spec that minimizes perceived latency.

Accessibility review notes:
- Use button element with aria-expanded and aria-controls for toggles.
- Screen reader announce pattern: "Cost details for <route> expanded". Provide SR-only summary when entering full-expanded state.
- Ensure 4.5:1 contrast for labels and interactive affordances in collapsed and summary states.
- Keyboard focus order defined: toggle -> quick-actions -> chart controls.

Redlines and visual polish requested:
- Reduce collapse/expand duration from 300ms to 200ms and prefer height-clip + fade to limit layout jank. Engineering will attempt CSS transform trick to avoid main-thread reflow.
- Update microcopy on quick-action icons (hover + tooltip) to be explicit: "Pin to dashboard", "Compare last 7 days", "Open attribution".
- Replace gray token used for background with semantic token surface-muted to align with dark mode.
- Add 2px focus ring with brand-accent on keyboard focus.

Decisions made during review:
1) Keep three microstates; full-expanded will lazy-load the heavy attribution chart after a 120ms debounce.
2) Toggle implemented as a single button controlling height clip + crossfade; chart container will reserve max-height to avoid large reflows where possible.
3) Add an explicit "estimated" pill (small label) shown when sampling or UV-smoothing is applied to the metric.
4) Motion spec: 200ms ease-out for expand, 140ms ease-in for collapse; no motion for quick toggles under 120ms.

Open items / Blockers:
- ENG-8899: Engineering to validate memory footprint with staging dataset (200+ tiles). Blocker for rollout beyond canary.
- Accessibility QA: Need a11y engineer pass and NVDA/VoiceOver checklists.
- Legal/marketing copy review for the "estimated" label language.

Next steps & handoff artifacts:
- Deliver redline frames in Figma and export component tokens (spacing, radii, colors). Link to redlines: https://redwood.design/figma/foldable-cost-tiles/redlines.
- Motion spec doc (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.com/display/UX/Foldable+Cost+Tiles+Motion+Spec
- Engineering PR template: https://github.com/redwood/repo/pulls (PR: ENG-PR-7804).
- QA checklist (included below).

QA checklist (for engineers & QA):
- [ ] Toggle keyboard focus order verified (Tab / Shift+Tab).
- [ ] Screen reader announces expansion state and provides SR-only summary.
- [ ] Expand/collapse animation timing matches motion spec.
- [ ] Lazy-loaded chart renders within 300ms after expand on staging infra.
- [ ] No layout shift > CLS 0.05 on pages with 20 tiles, and acceptable degradation with 200 tiles (SRE to sign off).
- [ ] Estimated pill shows for sampled windows; copying export includes annotation.

Metrics for rollout:
- Engagement: % of tiles expanded per session (baseline expected ~8%).
- Time-to-first-interaction: median time to expand after page load.
- Performance guardrails: 95th percentile expand latency < 250ms (chart render), CLS < 0.05 for pages with <= 50 tiles.

Notes for handoff: Please reference the Figma component "Foldable Cost Tile / v2" and the CSS token map. Engineering should label the feature gate as console.feature.foldable_cost_tiles to enable canary A/B.

title: Stacked header collapse and inline density toggle for responsive virtual table
preview: Context: As we expand the virtualized table component to support denser dashboards and narrower layouts, product teams keep requesting: (1) a robust way to display multi-level column headers without consuming vertical real estate, and (2) an easy inline control for users to switch density without visiting a separate settings panel. This ticket proposes: a stacked header pattern that can collapse into a single-line header per column on demand, paired with an inline density toggle (Compact / Comfortable / Readable) that persists per-user and integrates with our design tokens. Goals: reduce header height for dense views, maintain discoverability of grouped columns, keep keyboard and screen-reader accessibility, and avoid regressions in virtualization performance.

title: Reproducible customer-environment load harness for Private/VPC/on-prem
preview: Build a deterministic, customer-environment-aware load and replay harness for Private/VPC/on-prem deployments that produces reproducible tokens/sec and latency profiles (p50/p95/p99) and supports automated regression detection. The harness must capture environment metadata (OS/kernel, CUDA/cuDNN, driver, container runtime, CPU topology), record and replay request traces with KV-cache warmup behavior, support synthetic traffic shaping, and integrate with Prometheus/Grafana and Redwood Console for per-run comparisons.

title: Perturbation-driven quant impact orchestrator and threshold auto-calibrator
preview:
Goal: Build an orchestrator that runs targeted perturbation experiments whenever a quantization profile, model variant (AWQ/GPTQ), or runtime config changes and then auto-calibrates per-route thresholds used by the rollout gate. We want to detect semantic quality regressions for int8/int4 modes and recommend automatic or manual rollbacks. This should be safe to run on CI for PRs and as a lightweight smoke suite in staging and can be scheduled for periodic drift scans in production cohorts.

Scope: 1) A perturbation job generator that synthesizes small, semantically-relevant perturbations (paraphrase substitutions, token deletions, numeric edge cases) per route and interrogates the quantized model variant. 2) A scoring pipeline that computes a normalized quality delta across metrics (exact-match when applicable, embedding cosine on reference responses, reranker-based rank-drop, and proprietary semantic similarity score). 3) A Bayesian threshold auto-calibrator that recommends per-route tolerances and a global budget; stores output in the manifest used by rollout gates. 4) Orchestration glue to run experiments on PRs, scheduled runs, and on-demand from Console with links to dashboards and diffs.

Non-goals: This ticket does not implement the rollout plumbing itself or production forced rollbacks — those are separate (see ENG-771890). It also does not add new observability UIs beyond links to the Grafana panels created by the pipeline.

title: Normalize DST and plan-change hourly usage attribution to prevent negative invoice adjustments
preview:
Background:
Customers in EMEA/APAC saw sporadic negative invoice line items and duplicate credit adjustments for hourly metered plans after DST transitions or when a customer changed plan tiers mid-billing-cycle. Root cause appears to be a mismatch between: 1) how the hourly-aggregation batcher buckets events (UTC vs tenant-local timezone offsets during DST transitions), 2) the proration logic that splits usage across plan versions, and 3) the NetSuite/Stripe export deduplication which assumes stable invoice boundary alignment.

Goals / Acceptance Criteria:
- Hourly usage aggregation buckets align deterministically for customers in DST-observing timezones so that a DST forward/backward shift does not create overlapping/empty hour buckets.
- When a customer changes plan mid-cycle, prorated usage attribution must be split by effective-plan-interval boundaries derived from the customer's plan-change event timestamp (tenant-local) and mapped to the exact usage buckets without creating negative adjustments.
- Billing exports to Stripe and NetSuite must remain idempotent: repeated runs should not create duplicate credit/refund items nor negative-line reconciliation entries.
- New reconciler unit-tests and an integration run against a snapshot of past problematic customer data should demonstrate no negative adjustments for the sample set.

Proposed solution summary:
1) Introduce a DST-aware bucketing layer in the hourly-usage rollup job that normalizes to customer-local timezone offsets at the start of each billing cycle, storing both UTC bucket keys and the resolved offset used for bucketing. The job will explicitly annotate bucket metadata with the offset and a DST flag.
2) Modify proration logic to compute split factors using the same resolved offsets and to avoid 'inverse proration' when switch timestamps fall exactly on a bucket boundary by applying a deterministic tie-breaker (plan-change considered effective at bucket end).
3) Extend export pipeline to include an export-run fingerprint (run_id, resolved-offset, bill-cycle) and reconcile against previous export fingerprints to skip or merge idempotent changes instead of emitting inverse negative line items.
4) Add a backfill job to reprocess invoices for the small cache of affected customers during the last 6 months and emit adjusting invoice memos (not negative-line reversals) where appropriate.

Implementation notes:
- New schema fields on usage_buckets: "resolved_offset" (string), "dst_applied" (true/false as string), "bucket_key_v2" (string). We will index by bucket_key_v2 for fast lookups.
- Proration helper will be refactored into billing/proration_service.go with explicit dependency injection for timezone resolver to make unit testing easier.
- Export dedupe: add export_runs table with unique constraint on (customer_id, bill_cycle, resolved_offset, run_id) to guard against double exports.
- Backfill will run with low priority and rate-limit to avoid spike on billing DB.

Testing plan:
- Unit tests for timezone resolution across DST boundaries (US/EU/AU examples).
- Property-based test that simulates random plan-change timestamps across DST windows and asserts no negative net adjustments after proration.
- Integration test: run the updated reconciler against the shard of historical usage containing previously failing customers (identified in incident-1529).
- End-to-end QA: generate a synthetic customer with plan switch at DST transition, run export, and inspect Stripe export JSON to confirm no negative adjustment lines and correct proration amounts.

Rollout and monitoring:
- Feature-flag the new bucketing logic (billing.bucket_v2) and default off for 48h smoke tests on staging.
- Gradually enable for 5% of customers, monitor invoice diff rate, refund/credit incidents, and export idempotency metrics for 7 days.
- Dashboards to monitor: invoice_negative_line_rate, export_retry_count, proration_mismatch_rate.
- Rollback: flip feature flag and re-run export cleanup job if a regression is observed.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Backfill could create many adjustments; mitigation: force memos instead of negative-line refunds for safety and manual review for high-value accounts.
- Risk: Timezone resolver edge-cases (recent tzdb changes). Mitigation: pin tzdb version in billing service and include automated tzdb update test in CI.

Notes on scope and follow-ups:
- This ticket focuses on hourly bucketing & proration path only. Separate follow-ups will: 1) extend same logic to per-second burst metering, 2) harmonize with quota-offset refunds for rate-limit credits, 3) update customer-facing docs about how plan changes across DST are prorated.

title: Heartbeat admission lease failover to rescue dispatcher stalls and long-context tail
preview: Summary: We received three customer escalations (Hosted: low, Dedicated: high, Private: high) describing periodic p99+/tail latency spikes correlated with long-context sequences. Investigation pointed to a rare dispatcher stall where admission leases for short/latency-sensitive requests were not being renewed under mixed-length waves, causing queue starvation for small requests and batch amplification for long requests. Root cause hypothesis: an instance-level admission lease state was allowed to lapse when the heartbeat renewal path raced with a KV cache compaction event and an internal prefetch quarantine, producing a transient token that left the admission window closed until the next scheduler tick.

title: Policy exemption transitive fanout causing unmetered requests
preview: Summary: We observed a class of requests that bypass metering when a per-route exemption includes an alias/wildcard and the request fanouts to multiple internal sub-routes. The policy evaluator memoizes exemption results keyed insufficiently, causing exemptions to be applied transitively to child routes. This results in missing meter events and underbilling for affected orgs during fanout-heavy workloads.

title: VPC/On-Prem Upgrade Safeguard Coordinator — LTS rollout & orchestrated migrations
preview:
Goal: Build a resilient upgrade coordinator for Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem) that manages LTS channels, executes orchestrated migrations, and enforces rollout gates and rollback semantics. The coordinator will be an operator-style control plane component (controller+workers) that consumes an upgrade plan, performs preflight checks, schedules batched canaries, runs migrations (schema, filesystem, config), verifies postflight health, and either advances the rollout or triggers an automated rollback.

Scope:
- LTS channel awareness: mapping of releases to LTS channels and migration policies (forced vs opt-in).
- Upgrade plan model: DAG of steps (preflight -> canary cohorts -> data migrations -> global rollout) with idempotency and durable checkpoints.
- Orchestrated migrations: support for DB schema migrations (transactional where possible), KV cache migrations, and operator CRD version transitions.
- Rollout gates: health probes, smoke tests, performance SLO checks, and customer-visible feature flags gating.
- Rollback & safety: automatic snapshotting/backups before destructive steps, non-destructive dry-run mode, and full rollback playbook integration.
- Integrations: KMS for secrets, customer backup hooks, Console dashboards, and Console runbooks.
- Private constraints: offline air-gapped considerations, minimal network dependencies, and CLI-driven plan execution for on-prem installs.

Non-goals: CI build tooling, hosted-only features, and changes to runtime kernel selection (those are handled by serving teams).
